coredata.dc:title: A new ratiometric and colorimetric fluorescent Th4+ probe under extreme acidity and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Bo
Tan, Yanhong
Hu, Qinghua
Wang, Yuyuan
Mao, Yu
Tao, Peng
Wang, Hongqing
coredata.dc:description: It is always a significant challenge to develop a probe for Th4+ in the environment and in vivo under extreme acidity conditions, especially on real-time in the field. Herein, a new Th4+ fluorescent probe N1′,N2′-bis((3-hydroxytetrastyrene-2yl)methylene malonohydrazide (BH-TPE) was designed and studied. It could realize to detect Th4+ in a ratiometric way, which showed relative strong blue fluorescence at 450 nm and yellow-green fluorescence at 522 nm in the absence or present of Th4+, respectively, with good selectivity and sensitivity under extreme acidity environment. And the value of I522 /I450 of the probe had good linear relationship with Th4+ ion concentration in the range of 0–10 μM and the low detection limit 2 × 10−8 M. Meanwhile, it could monitor Th4+ ion by “naked eye” in the field on real-time due to the BH-TPE solution changed from colorless to light green with the increase of Th4+. Moreover, the possible fluorescent mechanism was proposed. Most importantly, the viability of BH-TPE to Th4+ ion was successfully applied to live cell imaging and environmental sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Tetraphenylethene
Th4+
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N1′, N2′-bis ((3-hydroxytetrastyrene-2 yl)methylene) malonohydrazide (BH-TPE)
Ability of recognition under extreme acidity conditions
Cytotoxicity testing and live cell imaging in extreme acidity
Comparison of present work with other sensors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Mechanism for detection of Th4+ ion by BH-TPE.
(a).The I522/I450 of BH-TPE and BH-TPE-Th4+ in various CH3OH/water mixtures; (b).The change of I522/I450 values of BH-TPE (10 μM) and BH-TPE(10 μM)+Th4+ (10 μM) at different pH.
(a). Fluorescent intensity of BH-TPE (10 μM) at pH from 2.0 to 9.0;(b).The ESIPT process of BH-TPE.

(a). UV–vis spectra of BH-TPE (10 μM) in the absence and presence of different metal ions (10 μM); (b). FL spectra of BH-TPE (10 μM) in the absence and presence of different metal ions (10 μM); (c). Fluorescence change upon addition of various metal ions (10 μM) to BH-TPE(10 μM) under the UV light; (d). The I522/I450 of BH-TPE (10 μM) in the presence of various metal ions (20 μM) and the BH-TPE + Th4+ ions (10 μM) in the presence of various metal ions (20 μM).
(a). Change of the emission intensity of BH-TPE (10 μM) with increasing Th4+ concentration (0–20 μM); (b). The linear relation between the I522/I450 of BH-TPE and Th4+ concentration (0–10 μM).
(a). Job plot of Th4+ complex formation [Th4+]/ [Th4++BH-TPE] was the molar fraction of Th4+ ion; (b). Benesi-Hildebrand plot of BH-TPE (10 μM) in the presence of Th4+ (0–1.0 equiv.).
1H NMR of the probe in d6-DMSO upon addition of 1 equiv. of Th4+ .
The HOMO-LUMO energy gaps for the BH-TPE and BH-TPE + Th4++H2O + NO3 − .
Fluorescent images of HeLa cells; BH-TPE treated (10 μM) cells; BH-TPE (10 μM)+Th4+ (5 μM) treated cells; BH-TPE (10 μM)+Th4+ (10 μM) treated cells. Scale bar =40 μm.
The results of Th4+ determination in Xiang Jiang River water samples.
Comparison of present work with literature reports (/= Not Mentioned).

head.ce:title: A new ratiometric and colorimetric fluorescent Th4+ probe under extreme acidity and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory for the Design and Application of Actinide Complexes, University of South China, 28 Changsheng West Road, Hengyang, Hunan, 421001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory for the Design and Application of Actinide Complexes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan key laboratory for the design and application of actinide complexes, University of South China, 28 Changsheng West Road, Hengyang, Hunan, 421001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, 28 Changsheng West Road, Hengyang, Hunan, 421001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, 28 Changsheng West Road, Hengyang, Hunan, 421001, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is always a significant challenge to develop a probe for Th4+ in the environment and in vivo under extreme acidity conditions, especially on real-time in the field. Herein, a new Th4+ fluorescent probe N1′,N2′-bis((3-hydroxytetrastyrene-2yl)methylene malonohydrazide (BH-TPE) was designed and studied. It could realize to detect Th4+ in a ratiometric way, which showed relative strong blue fluorescence at 450 nm and yellow-green fluorescence at 522 nm in the absence or present of Th4+, respectively, with good selectivity and sensitivity under extreme acidity environment. And the value of I522 /I450 of the probe had good linear relationship with Th4+ ion concentration in the range of 0–10 μM and the low detection limit 2 × 10−8 M. Meanwhile, it could monitor Th4+ ion by “naked eye” in the field on real-time due to the BH-TPE solution changed from colorless to light green with the increase of Th4+. Moreover, the possible fluorescent mechanism was proposed. Most importantly, the viability of BH-TPE to Th4+ ion was successfully applied to live cell imaging and environmental sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Thorium as fuel is widely used in the nuclear industry [1]. However, with the development of the nuclear industry, thorium usually easy enters in industrial wastewater, which will cause a serious threat to human health and the environment if without properly treated [2–4]. Thorium usually exists in the form of Th4+ in the wastewater of the nuclear industry, because most of the reprocessing of nuclear fuel are carried out in medium with high acidity [5–7]. On the other hand, the pH in vivo rang from alkaline to highly acidic levels for different cells, subcellular organelles, intercellular matrices as well as body fluids [8], for example, pH value was as low as 1.0 to 3.5 in the stomach. Therefore, it is significant to detect Th4+ ion under extreme acidity not only in environments but also in the living cell. At present, the fluorescence analysis method has attracted more attentions because of the advantages of simpleness, rapidity, highly selectivity [9–14]. Although there were some reports about Th4+ ion probes [15–23]. None of the fluorescence probes could detect thorium in the environment and living cell under extremely acidic conditions (for example, pH less than 3.0). More impressively, they were not only unable to recognize Th4+ ion by naked eyes, but also could not eliminate the influence of main interference sources of UO2 2+,V4+, Fe3+ and Ga3+ [16]. Importantly, all of them depended only on the enhancement or weakening of the fluorescence intensity, which would be significantly affected by the instrument efficiency and environmental conditions such as variation of chemosensor concentration, background fluorescence, and photobleaching [24–26]. Compared with these probes, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor could provide a built-in calibration for the environment by recording two emissions ratio signals at different wavelengths, which could effectively provide sensitivity and improve measurement accuracy [27–29]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a ratiometric fluorescence probe to detect Th4+ ion, which could apply in the environmental sample and living cell, with good selectivity and high sensitivity under strongly acidic conditions.

Salicylaldehyde Schiff-base [30] usually showed dual emissions and was widely used for ratiometric probes development by its good photo-stability, excellent light-up ratio and large Stokes shift. Also, it showed unique polarity/pH-dependent optical properties in high acidic condition and metal ion responsive fluorescence [30], which indicated their potential prospects in metal ion sensing in acidic condition.

For the sake of the above mentioned, we herein reported a new Tetraphenyl-salicylaldehyde Schiff base (BH-TPE) as a Th4+ ion fluorescent probe (Scheme 1 ). It exhibited extremely high selectivity and sensitivity for Th4+ ion and could monitor Th4+ ion in the field on real-time using observing the color change from colorless to light green by “naked eye” under extreme acidity conditions. Furthermore, it has also realized to detect Th4+ ion in solution and living cell.

All chemicals were purchased from Aladdin and used without further purification. Nitrate of different metal were dissolved in distilled water to prepare various metal ions solutions (Dy3+, Eu3+, Zr4+, La3+, Sm3+, Ag+, UO2 2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Th4+).

Absorption spectra were recorded by a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). A Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used to measuring fluorescent spectra data. NMR spectra were obtained on a Bruker 500 MHz spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). A model pHs-3C digital pH/mV meter (Shanghai, China) was used for recording the value of pH. Nikon TE2000 inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon, Japan) was used for the cell fluorescent images.

Fluorescence and absorption measurements were performed in the following steps: adding a certain amount of metal ions (1 × 10−3M, H2O) and an appropriate amount of BH-TPE (1 × 10−3M, CH3OH) solution to the 5 mL colorimetric tube, then diluted with CH3OH/H2O to the mark (CH3OH:H2O,v/v = 7:3, pH = 2.0). The absorption and fluorescence of the solution were run after 10 min at room temperature. The excitation and emission slit width of fluorescence measurements were both 5 nm, and the excitation wavelength was 320 nm.

Propanedioyl dihydrazide (Compound 1) and (2-hydroxy-5-(4, 4, 5, 5-tetramethyl [1,3,2] dioxaborolan-2-yl)-benzaldehyde (Compound 2) were synthesized according to the literature [22] and [31], respectively. A detailed procedure for the synthesis of BH-TPE was shown in Scheme 1.

Under a nitrogen atmosphere, 2-hydroxy-5-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl [1,3,2]dioxaborolan-2-yl)-benzaldehyde(1.86, 7.5 mmol) and bromotriphenylethylene (1.65, 5.0 mmol) were added to a mixture of toluene (15 mL), TBTA(0.17 g, 0.5 mmol) and 2 M potassium carbonate aqueous solution(9 ml). Pd (PPh3)4 (15 mg, 1.31.3 × 10−2 mmol) was added after the mixture stirred for 0.5 h at room temperature. Then, the mixture was heated to 100℃ for 16 h under N2. And the product was poured into water and extracted three times with dichloromethane. The obtained organic layer was dried over anhydrous Na2SO4, concentrated via rotary evaporation and purified using column chromatography (petroleum ether/dichloromethane (5:1,v/v). Finally, a yellow solid was obtained (0.93 g, 75%). 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ ppm 10.71 (s, 1 H), 10.13 (s, 1 H), 7.25(d, 1 H), 7.14 (m, 10 H), 7.01 (t, 6 H), 6.77 (d, 1 H). FTIR (KBr):3614, 3515, 3074, 3018, 2835, 2727, 1662, 1581, 1477, 1442, 1282, 1205, 1163, 752, 696, 613, 575 cm−1; MS (ESI): m/z(%):Calculated for C27H20O2, 376.1(M), found 375.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N1′, N2′-bis ((3-hydroxytetrastyrene-2 yl)methylene) malonohydrazide (BH-TPE)
ce:section.ce:para: Compound 3 (376 mg, 1 mmol) was dissolved in 30 mL ethanol, and then added to a two-necked flask which contained propanedioyl dihydrazide (66 mg). After refluxing for 4 h, the solution was cooled to room temperature, filtrated to give crude product. Then, recrystallized with ethanol, a light yellow solid BH-TPE was obtained. Yield :(0.375 g, 86%), m.p. 184.5–185.5 °C. 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ 12.18 (s,2 H), 11.67 (s, 2 H), 8.11 (s,2 H), 7.25 (d,J = 2.4 Hz,2 H), 7.21-7.11 (m, 20 H), 7.05-6.93 (m, 12 H), 6.77 (d,J = 8.5 Hz, 2 H), 3.70(s, 2 H). FTIR (KBr):3420, 3192, 3076, 3053, 3022, 1676, 1602, 1576, 1517, 1491, 1275, 698 cm−1; 13C NMR (101 MHz, DMSO, 25℃)δ = 169.02, 156.39, 155.43, 143.82, 143.50, 137.83, 131.66, 131.48, 131.21, 131.13, 131.07, 131.04, 131.00, 130.96, 129.63, 129.38, 129.27, 128.69, 128.35, 128.29, 128.22, 128.18, 127.10, 126.91, 126.77, 125.80, 119.79, 118.65, 31.43 ppm. MS(ESI):m/z(%):Calculated for C57H44N4O4, 848.34(M), found 849.37.

The fluorescence properties of a probe containing nitrogen and oxygen were dramatically affected by the water volume fraction of solvent and pH [27]. Thus the ratiometric fluorescence values (I522/I450) of BH-TPE solution in the absence of and existence of Th4+ were investigated by increasing the water content in CH3OH medium (Fig. 1 a). From Fig. 1a, it was found that the fluorescence ratiometric value (I522/I450) increased clearly from the water content of 30% to 50%(for free BH-TPE) or 25% to 30%(for BH-TPE-Th4+ ). What’s more, the value of I522 /I450 reached the maximum at the water content of 30% for BH-TPE-Th4+ . Meanwhile, the effect of pH value on the pure probe was studied in the range of 2.0 to 9.0 (Fig. 2 a). As shown in Fig. 2a, only blue fluorescence emission at 450 nm(Enol FL) was observed when pH from 2.0 to 3.0 and 7.0 to 9.0, which was due to the intramolecular hydrogen bond of the probe was broken in strong acid or alkali conditions [27,32]. At pH 4.0–6.0, a new yellow-green fluorescence at 525 nm(ESIPT emission) arose, which was attributed to the ESIPT process (Fig. 2b) of BH-TPE [27,32]. Furthermore, the I522/I450 of BH-TPE and BH-TPE-Th4+ solutions were also obtained at different pH in the system of CH3OH:H2O (V/V = 7:3) (Fig. 1b). From the Fig. 1b, it could found that the I522/I450 of the BH-TPE did not show significant changes while the pH from 1.0 to 3.0 but increased gradually when the pH value was greater than 3.0. On the contrary, the I522/I450 of the BH-TPE-Th4+ remarkably augment while the pH from 1.0 to 3.0. Moreover, it was close between the pH 2.0 and 3.0 and achieved maximum at pH = 3.0. Also, while the pH value was greater than or equal to 5.0, the I522/I450 of BH-TPE was very close to that of BH-TPE-Th4+ . In consideration of the different species of thorium at different pH value, the software Visual MINTEQ3 was used to simulate the species distribution of thorium in the pH range from 1.0 to 7.0 (Fig. S4). It was found that the form of Th4+ exceeded more than 85% when the pH was less than 2.0, while mainly existed in the form of Th (OH)3 + and Th(OH)2 2+ when the pH was greater than 3.5. When the pH was greater than 5.0, the form of Th4+ was very few of all species of thorium. According to the above results, it was the species of Th4+ that made the change of fluorescence when thorium reacted with BH-TPE. Finally, pH = 2.0 was chosen for further research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ability of recognition under extreme acidity conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate BH-TPE ability of recognition for Th4+ under extremely acidic environment, UV and Fluorescence spectra of the probe to various metal ions were carried out in the system of CH3OH:H2O(V/V = 7:3, pH = 2.0), and the results were presented in Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption peak of the pure probe appeared at 288 nm, which was unchanged after adding 1.0 equiv. of Dy3+, Eu3+, Zr4+, La3+, Sm3+, Ag+, UO2 2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ to BH-TPE(10 μM) solutions. However, the absorption peak at 288 nm shifted to 320 nm with the addition of Th4+ ion, and the absorbance decreased. At the same time, the BH-TPE solution changed from colorless to light-green with the increase of Th4+ (Fig. 3a inset). Also, a new absorption peak was found at 410 nm after adding Cu2+. As shown in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b), the pure probe showed emission at 450 nm, but it disappeared and a new fluorescent emission near 522 nm appeared, while the solution changed from blue to yellow-green, after the addition of Th4+. Notably, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm would decrease accompanying with UO2 2+ and Cu2+ adding, but no new emission peak appeared at 522 nm. Excluding them, no other ions could change the fluorescence color (Fig. 3c).

To further verify the selectivity of BH-TPE, competitive experiments were performed by adding Th4+ ion (10 μM) to BH-TPE solution in the presence of other metal ions (50 μM) (Fig. 3d). It was discovered that the coexistent metal ions did not cause a significant change in the I522/I450 of BH-TPE except for Cu2+. However, its distractions could be masked by 1,10-Phenanthroline. Furthermore, the reversibility of complex BH-TPE-Th4+ was investigated through adding I −, F−, Br−, Cl−, Ac−, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, S2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, ClO4 −and EDTA (2 equiv.) to the solution of BH-TPE-Th4+ . But none of them could reverse the fluorescence of BH-TPE-Th4+ , which proved that these anions would not interfere with the recognition of Th4+ by BH-TPE.

Namely, BH-TPE could not only eliminate the interference of general metal ion, but also could remove the main interference of UO2 2+, V4+, Fe3+ and Ga3+. More amazedly, it has realized to recognize Th4+ under extreme acidity conditions.

In addition, the titration fluorescence spectrum of BH-TPE with Th4+ was shown in the Fig. 4 a. As the Th4+ increased from 0 to 20 μM, the fluorescence peak at 450 nm gradually diminished, and the emission at 522 nm gradually enhanced. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence value (I522/I450) exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of Th4+ in the range of 0 to 10 μM (Fig. 4b). The linear equation was y = 0.1359x+0.1822, R2 = 0.9724; the minimum detection limit was 2 × 10−8M calculated according to LOD = 3σ/k [22].

The binding mode of the BH-TPE and Th4+ was investigated by keeping the total concentration of BH-TPE and Th4+ constant (10 μM) and changing the molar fraction of Th4+ in CH3OH:H2O (v/v = 7:3, pH = 2.0). The Job’s plot was given in Fig. 5 a. When the mole fraction was 0.5, the value of I522/I450 was the largest, which indicated that the complex of BH-TPE-Th4+ was 1:1 stoichiometric. According to the Benesi-Hildebrand 1:1 mode equation
1/(F-F0) = 1/[[Th4+](Fmax-F0)Ka] + 1/(Fmax-F0)
where F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of BH-TPE, F was the fluorescence intensity of the BH-TPE-Th4+ , Fmax was the fluorescence intensity of the complete interaction of BH-TPE binding with Th4+ ion, all of the fluorescence intensity was at the emission at 522 nm. [Th4+] was the concentration of Th4+. As shown in Fig. 5b, the linear equation was y = 6.88428E-7x-0.03809, R2 = 0.9985, Ka = 2.031 × 104 M−1, which proved that the 1:1 binding mode was identical for the complex of BH-TPE-Th4+ . It was agreed with the Job’s plot completely.

To further understand the binding behavior, probe BH-TPE and Th4+ ion were further investigated by 1HNMR titration, IR spectroscopy and EIMS spectra. The 1HNMR titration spectra were shown in Fig. 6 . It was found that the pure probe BH-TPE exhibited a hydroxyl (−OH) signal at 12.20 ppm (a), an imine (NH) signal at 11.67 ppm (b) and a Schiff base (−HCN) signal at 8.12 ppm (c). After adding 1.0 equiv. of Th4+, the signal peaks at 11.67 ppm and 8.13 ppm were almost unchanged, and the signal peak at 12.20 ppm disappeared. It indicated that the O atom from OH (a) participated in coordination. From the IR spectra (Fig. S5), it observed that BH-TPE showed obvious peaks (ν, cm−1) at 3420(OH), 3192 (NH), 1676(CO), 1602(CN). But the peak at 3420 cm−1 disappeared, and the characteristic peaks at 1676 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1 moved to 1610 cm−1 and1537 cm-1 in IR spectroscopy of BH-TPE-Th4+ , respectively. In addition, the –NH peak at 3192 cm−1 still existed. At the same time, MS spectroscopy found the peak at m/z 1159.5 (Fig. S6) which belonged to [BH-TPE + Th4++NO3-+H2O] (theoretical value 1159.03). These above results proved that the both of CN, CO and −OH on BH-TPE participated in the coordination with Th4+ ion, while the –NH group did not. According to the results of structural characterizations, the binding mode was given in Scheme 2 .

According to the effect of pH, the single blue fluorescence of the probe at 450 nm was mainly due to the breakage of intramolecular hydrogen bond under extremely acidic conditions (pH around 2.0), which resulted in the block of ESIPT process. As for the enhanced yellow-green fluorescent emission at 522 nm with the increase of concentration of Th4+, it was ascribed to the chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) effect [33,34]. As mentioned above, the chelation between the Th4+ and BH-TPE occurred through nitrogen atom from CN and O atoms from CO, −OH, NO3 − and H2O group. It is precise because of this chelation that improved the rigidity of BH-TPE, which favored a more planar and conjugated structure, leading to the enhanced fluorescent intensity.

In order to further understand the relationship between photophysical properties and chelation of probe BH-TPE with Th4+, computations on the probe before and after chelate with Th4+ were performed based on density functional theory (DFT). The optimized molecular structure of BH-TPE and BH-TPE-Th4+ was obtained by the Gaussian09 program DFT at the level of B3LYP/6–31 G (d) (Fig. S7). Several coordination modes were discussed in the calculation such as 1:1, 1:2, 2:1 and others. It was found that only the 1:1 mode could achieve system convergence and reach a stable state, which was agreed with the job curve. As shown in Fig. 7 , it was discussed what was the quantum characters of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of BH-TPE and BH-TPE-Th4+ by DFT calculation. It could be found that the electrons cloud on the HOMO of BH-TPE mainly distributed on the tetraphenylethene group. While excited, on the LUMO of BH-TPE, only part of the electrons cloud transfer to the malonic acid dihydrazide group and most of the atoms were noncoplanar. Also, the electrons cloud of BH-TPE-Th4+ on the HOMO and LUMO was mainly located on Th4+ ion coordinated malonic acid dihydrazide-Schiff base structure and most of the atoms are coplanar, which might enhance rigidity and also improved the conjugation, due to the CHEF effect. What’s more, the calculated energy gaps between HOMO and LUMO were decreased from BH-TPE (3.9448 eV) to its Th4+ complexes BH-TPE-Th4+ (0.5150 eV), which was consistent with both the UV–vis spectrum and emission enhancement and redshift of BH-TPE-Th4+ complex [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity testing and live cell imaging in extreme acidity
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the biocompatibility of BH-TPE, the cytotoxicity of BH-TPE was determined by MTT assay using the HeLa cells. All steps of cell culture were according to the literature [8]. There was no obvious change in cell proliferation and the cell survival rate exceeded 90% after cultured with different concentration of BH-TPE from 0 μM to 50 μM for 48 h (Fig. S8). It indicated that BH-TPE had very low cytotoxicity and had the potential value of medical treatment and biological research for detecting Th4+ ion in the living cell [36,37].

HeLa cells were incubated with BH-TPE (10 μM in HCl solutions contains 1% DMSO) for 30 min at 37℃, followed by addition of Th4+ ion (5–10 μM) and then incubated for another 30 min. Next, the cells were washed three times with HCl solutions to remove the excess of the probe. As shown in Fig. 8 , the cells incubated with BH-TPE showed strong blue fluorescence in the Blue channel, but no fluorescence in the Green channel. After adding 0.5–1.0 equiv. Th4+ ion to BH-TPE-treated HeLa cells, the blue fluorescence of the in the Blue channel was weakened, and the yellow-green fluorescence was gradually enhanced in the Green channel. These results proved that the probe could detect Th4+ ion in living cells under extremely acidic conditions by the ratiometric way.

To verify the practical application of the probe, Xiang Jiang River water was used to prepare different concentrations of Th4+ ion solutions. And the concentration of Th4+ ion was determined by fluorescence spectrophotometry. Table 1 showed that the probe BH-TPE provided very satisfactory results for the determination of Th4+ in Xiang Jiang River water samples. Thus, the probe might have the potential to detect water pollution near thorium mining facilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of present work with other sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The comparison of probe BH-TPE with other Th4+ ion probes was listed in Table 2 . It was found that BH-TPE was the first ratio fluorescent probe and it was the first time to explain the effect of species of thorium on fluorescence. And the BH-TPE established a lower detection limit and good selectivity to Th4+. More importantly, the probe BH-TPE could be used not only for quantitative detection but also for Th4+ on-line monitoring. More surprisedly, it could be realized to recognize Th4+ in living cells under extreme acidity conditions.

In summary, a ratiometric Th4+ fluorescence and colorimetric sensor (BH-TPE), which emitted a single blue fluorescence at 450 nm without Th4+ ion due to the fracture of intramolecular hydrogen bond under strong acidic conditions (pH around 2.0) and displayed a new yellow-green fluorescent emission at 522 nm through the chelating enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) processes in the present of Th4+ species, was first reported in this work. More impressively, the BH-TPE has realized to recognize Th4+ by fluorescence and to monitor Th4+ ion by “naked eye” under extreme acidity environment without the interference of general metal ion and special metal ion(UO2 2+, V4+, Fe3+ and Ga3+). And the value of I522 /I450 of the probe had a good linear relationship with Th4+ ion concentration in the range of 0 to 10 μM with LOD 2 × 10−8 M. Also, the formation of a 1:1 complex of BH-TPE and Th4+ was established by 1HNMR titration, IR spectroscopy, EIMS analysis and DFT calculation. What’s more, structural characterizations indicated that the O atom from −OH and CO, the N atom from CN participated coordination between BH-TPE and Th4+ ion to form a complex. More importantly, the BH-TPE could be used not only for Th4+ ion detection in actual water samples, but also for cell imaging in acidic conditions. Therefore, it has potential applications in environmental and biological systems for thorium ion detection.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11875161 and 11804146); Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2016JJ5003); the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (No. 17B223); the Program of Innovative Research for Postgraduate of Hunan Province [CX2018B595].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.126675.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective extraction, preconcentration and transport studies of thorium(IV) using octa-functionalized calix[4]resorcinarene-hydroxamic acid
Anal. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.K Jain, S.G. Pillai, R.A. Pandya, Y.K.Agrawal, P.S Shrivastav. Selective Extraction, Preconcentration and Transport Studies of Thorium(IV) Using Octa-Functionalized Calix[4]resorcinarene-Hydroxamic Acid[J]. Analytical Sciences, 2005, 21(2):129-135. DOI:10.2116/analsci.21.129

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hazards in the Chemical Laboratory

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.D. Muir.Hazards in the Chemical Laboratory. 2nd ed. The Chem. Soc. 1977.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bo Liu, A third-grade graduate student of College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, University of South China. His research interests focus mainly on the field of optical sensors.
Yanhong Tan, A third-grade graduate student of College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, University of South China, her research centers on optical sensors.

Hongqing Wang, received his PhD in 2004 from College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, China. He working in School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, University of South China as a professor. His current research interests are focusing on the chemistry of functional complexes, materials as well as its applications of environmental engineering and living cell.

Qinghua Hu, obtained his PhD degree at the College of Materials Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China in 2016. He is now working in University of South China as a lecturer. His research interests include materials synthesis, sensors and fluorescence spectroscopy for their biological applications.

Yuyuan Wang, A lecturer of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, University of South China. His research interests include materials synthesis, sensors and the chemistry of functional complexes.

Yu Mao,obtained his PhD degree at the College of Queen's University Belfast,He is now working in University of South China as a lecturer. His research interests is Theoretical calculation of chemical structures.
Peng Tao, A third-grade graduate student of College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, University of South China, her research centers on optical sensors.
